maml:description.maml:para: Get the primary display size in pixels.
When positioning the PSClock, or any WPF form, you might need to know the dimension of your display.

command:example.maml:title: -------------------------- Example 1 --------------------------
command:example.dev:code:
PS C:\> Get-PrimaryDisplaySize
Width Height WorkArea ----- ------ -------- 1536.00 864.00 0,0,1536,816

dev:remarks.maml:para: These values are different than $host.UI.RawUI.WindowSize.Width and $host.UI.RawUI.WindowSize.Height. The $host values are dimensions of the console window, not the display screen.

command:example.maml:title: -------------------------- Example 2 --------------------------
command:example.dev:code:
PS C:\> Get-PrimaryDisplaySize | Select-Object -ExpandProperty WorkArea
IsEmpty : False Location : 0,0 Size : 1536,816 X : 0 Y : 0 Width : 1536 Height : 816 Left : 0 Top : 0 Right : 1536 Bottom : 816 TopLeft : 0,0 TopRight : 1536,0 BottomLeft : 0,816 BottomRight : 1536,816

maml:description.maml:para: Get PSClock details.
This command will provide detailed information about a PSClock.

command:example.maml:title: -------------------------- Example 1 --------------------------
command:example.dev:code:
PS C:\> Get-PSClock
Running Format FontFamily Size Weight Color Style OnTop RunspaceID ------- ------ ---------- ---- ------ ----- ----- ----- ---------- True F Candara 50 Normal Honeydew Normal False 7

dev:remarks.maml:para: Get details about the currently running clock.

command:example.maml:title: -------------------------- Example 2 --------------------------
command:example.dev:code:
PS C:\> Get-PSClock | Select *
Started : 5/4/2026 10:44:48 PM Format : F Output : Thursday, May 7, 2026 3:27:40 PM Running : True FontFamily : Candara Size : 50 Weight : Normal Color : Honeydew Style : Normal OnTop : False CurrentPosition : {61, 62} RunspaceID : 7

dev:remarks.maml:para: Get all details about the current clock.

maml:description.maml:para: Save current PSClock settings to a file.

This command will export the settings for a PSClock to an xml file,using Export-CliXML. The file PSClockSettings.xml will be created in $HOME. The next time you run Start-PSClock, if this file is detected, the settings will be imported and used for the clock unless you use -Force. If you no longer wish to use the saved settings, you can manually delete the file.
The clock does not have to be running in order to export the settings.
Prompts you for confirmation before running the cmdlet.
Shows what would happen if the cmdlet runs. The cmdlet is not run.
Display the file with saved settings.
Prompts you for confirmation before running the cmdlet.
Shows what would happen if the cmdlet runs. The cmdlet is not run.
Display the file with saved settings.

dev:remarks.maml:para: Save current settings to $HOME\PSClockSettings.xml.

command:example.maml:title: -------------------------- Example 2 --------------------------
command:example.dev:code:
PS C:\> Save-PSClock -passthru
Directory: C:\Users\Jeff
Mode LastWriteTime Length Name ---- ------------- ------ ---- -a--- 3/30/2025 2:26 PM 1123 PSClockSettings.xml

dev:remarks.maml:para: Save current settings and display the file.

maml:description.maml:para: Modify a running PSClock.

Use this command to modify the settings of a running PSClock. You can also increase the size by selecting the clock and use the + key. Decrease using the - key. Each change takes a second to be applied. You might need to "grab" the clock and move it slightly to ensure you have it selected.
If you want to change the position, left-click and drag to re-position.

Specify a font color like Green or an HTML code like '#FF1257EA'. You can also use any [System.Drawing.Brushes] color like Coral or SkyBlue. If you press Ctrl+Space after the parameter, you can use PSReadLine to display options formatted in the corresponding color.
Specify a .NET format string value like F, or G.
Prompts you for confirmation before running the cmdlet.
Specify a font family.
How large do you want the font size?
Specify a font style. Accepted values: Normal, Italic, Oblique
Specify a font weight. Accepted values: 'Normal', 'Bold', 'Light', 'Medium', 'SemiBold'
Should the clock be on top of other applications?
Shows what would happen if the cmdlet runs. The cmdlet is not run.
Specify an array of (X,Y) coordinates for the clock position. Use Get-PrimaryDisplaySize to determine the values.

Specify a font color like Green or an HTML code like '#FF1257EA'. You can also use any [System.Drawing.Brushes] color like Coral or SkyBlue. If you press Ctrl+Space after the parameter, you can use PSReadLine to display options formatted in the corresponding color.
Prompts you for confirmation before running the cmdlet.
Specify a .NET format string value like F, or G.
Specify a font family.
How large do you want the font size?
Specify a font style. Accepted values: Normal, Italic, Oblique
Specify a font weight. Accepted values: 'Normal', 'Bold', 'Light', 'Medium', 'SemiBold'
Should the clock be on top of other applications?
Shows what would happen if the cmdlet runs. The cmdlet is not run.
Specify an array of (X,Y) coordinates for the clock position. Use Get-PrimaryDisplaySize to determine the values.

dev:remarks.maml:para: Adjust the font size and clock position. This example is using the Position parameter alias.

maml:description.maml:para: Show a font preview in a WPF form.

Use this command to display a preview of a font in a WPF form. This can be useful when you need to see what a font looks like before using it in a clock. You can use the buttons or arrow keys to navigate through the fonts. Press Ctrl+Q to quit or manually close the form.
The default text to display.
The default text to display.

dev:remarks.maml:para: Your prompt will be blocked until you close the form. This will display the default sample text from Get-Date -Format F.
Launch the preview form with the current date displayed. You can always modify the text in the text box.
Pipe a sample string to the command.

maml:description.maml:para: Show a GUI preview of PSClock settings.

Use this command to display a GUI preview of the settings that can be applied to a PSClock. The preview text will adjust to the selected font family, weight, style, color and size. Changes will be reflected in the preview box. Click the Apply button to commit the changes to the current clock. If you don't want to apply any changes, close the form. The form elements have tooltips to help you understand what each setting does. Hover your mouse over the element to see the tooltip.
You can also invoke this command by selecting the PSClock and pressing 'p'.
If you have a running clock, the form will default to the current clock settings. If you don't have a running clock, the form will use the default settings from Start-PSClock.

dev:remarks.maml:para: Your prompt will be blocked until you close the form.

maml:description.maml:para: Start a console-based clock.

Use this command to start a clock in your current PowerShell console or terminal window. The clock will be displayed in the upper right corner of your console window. You can format the output and even wrap it in a border.
While running, there is hashtable of settings stored as a global variable $consoleClockSettings.
ConsoleColor values may get converted to ANSI sequences. You won't see value for ANSI sequences because they are escape sequences. But you can change any value.
PS C:\> $consoleClockSettings.DisplayColor = "`e[91m"
PS C:\> $consoleClockSettings.Border = $False
These values will be immediately detected.
If you manually delete the variable, the clock will automatically be removed, although the last display will remain until you clear the host or it scrolls out of view.
You may see cursor flickering, especially is using a border. This is to be expected.

Specify the date time formatting using a .NET format string. For a list of available .NET format specifiers, see https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/standard/base-types/custom-date-and-time-format-strings
This format value is case-sensitive.
Specify an ANSI style or console color for the clock display.
Add a line border with rounded corners to the display. You can customize the border color using the -BorderColor parameter.
Specify an ANSI or console color for the border. This will only be used with -Border.
Shows what would happen if the cmdlet runs. The cmdlet is not run.
Prompts you for confirmation before running the cmdlet.

Specify the date time formatting using a .NET format string. For a list of available .NET format specifiers, see https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/standard/base-types/custom-date-and-time-format-strings
This format value is case-sensitive.
Specify an ANSI style or console color for the clock display.
Add a line border with rounded corners to the display. You can customize the border color using the -BorderColor parameter.
Specify an ANSI or console color for the border. This will only be used with -Border.
Shows what would happen if the cmdlet runs. The cmdlet is not run.
Prompts you for confirmation before running the cmdlet.

dev:remarks.maml:para: Start the default clock displayed in yellow. The default formatting will look like Thursday, May 7, 2026 3:34:51 PM.
Start a clock using the default border. The date time will be formatted as 5/7/2026 3:37 PM.
Start a console clock using $PSStyle to customize the appearance. The clock is formatted to display the time in 24-hour format.
Start the clock using a colored border and styled content.

maml:description.maml:para: Start a PSClock.

Start a WPF-based PSClock that will run in a background runspace. The clock will be displayed in the center of the screen. You can click and drag the clock to reposition using the left mouse button. You might have to try a few times to "grab" the clock. You can close the clock with a right-click or the Stop-PSClock command.
The command lets you specify any datetime format string. This is the same value you would use in a command like Get-Date -format U. Note that these strings are case-sensitive.
The clock runs in a separate runspace launched from your PowerShell session. If you close the session, the clock will also be closed.
The command is designed to only have one clock running at a time. If you try to start another clock from another PowerShell session, you will get a warning.
If you have saved PSClock settings, the exported values will be used unless you use -Force. The saved settings file will not be deleted.
Specify a .NET format string value like F, or G. See https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/standard/base-types/custom-date-and-time-format-strings for more information.
This value is case-sensitive.
Specify a font color like Green or an HTML code like '#FF1257EA'. You can also use any [System.Drawing.Brushes] color like Coral or SkyBlue.
Specify a font family.
Specify a font size.
Specify a font style. Accepted values: Normal, Italic, Oblique
Specify a font weight. Accepted values: 'Normal', 'Bold', 'Light', 'Medium', 'SemiBold'
Do you want the clock to always be on top?
Force a new PSClock, ignoring any previously saved settings. The saved settings file will remain.
Specify the clock position as an array of left and top values.
Specify a font color like Green or an HTML code like '#FF1257EA'. You can also use any [System.Drawing.Brushes] color like Coral or SkyBlue.
Specify a .NET format string value like F, or G. See https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/standard/base-types/custom-date-and-time-format-strings for more information.
This value is case-sensitive.
Specify a font family.
Specify a font size.
Specify a font style. Accepted values: Normal, Italic, Oblique
Specify a font weight. Accepted values: 'Normal', 'Bold', 'Light', 'Medium', 'SemiBold'
Do you want the clock to always be on top?
Force a new PSClock, ignoring any previously saved settings. The saved settings file will remain.
Specify the clock position as an array of left and top values.

dev:remarks.maml:para: Start a new PSClock using the default parameter values. If you have saved PSClock settings, then those values will be used.
Start a clock using specific font settings. The clock will be displayed center screen. It will use the default datetime format string.

maml:description.maml:para: Start a clock in the session title.

Start a clock in the title window or tab of your current PowerShell session. This works best in Windows Terminal. Although you may need to configure the Windows Terminal tab width to use the title size, especially if you decide to include the current location. The change won't take effect until you restart Windows Terminal.
You can also choose to display additional information such as the PowerShell version, your current location, a static bit of text, or the value of a global variable.
Once the clock is running, if you would like to change its appearance, you can modify settings in the global titleClockSettings variable.

Specify the date time formatting using a .NET format string. For a list of available .NET format specifiers, see https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/standard/base-types/custom-date-and-time-format-strings
This format value is case-sensitive. The parameter is not technically mandatory, but it is required.
Show the current location. This might display a long path. If using Windows Terminal you will want to configure tabs to use the title length.
Show the PowerShell Version number.
Specify the separator character.
Display a static text message. This should be a short note like "Admin" or "Dev".
Display a variable value. Enter the variable name without the $ prefix.
Shows what would happen if the cmdlet runs. The cmdlet is not run.
Prompts you for confirmation before running the cmdlet.

Specify the date time formatting using a .NET format string. For a list of available .NET format specifiers, see https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/standard/base-types/custom-date-and-time-format-strings
This format value is case-sensitive. The parameter is not technically mandatory, but it is required.
Show the current location. This might display a long path. If using Windows Terminal you will want to configure tabs to use the title length.
Show the PowerShell Version number.
Specify the separator character.
Display a static text message. This should be a short note like "Admin" or "Dev".
Display a variable value. Enter the variable name without the $ prefix.
Shows what would happen if the cmdlet runs. The cmdlet is not run.
Prompts you for confirmation before running the cmdlet.

dev:remarks.maml:para: Start the clock in the console title or window tab. This will display the current time like 8:24:22 AM
This will display a clock with additional information that looks like 5/11/2026 8:30:11 AM | 7.6.1 | Admin
Start a title clock using the tilde character as the separator with the default date time format. The clock will look like 5/11/2026 8:35:26 AM ~ 7.6.1 ~ NoProfile.

maml:description.maml:para: Stop a running console clock.
Use this command to stop a running console clock. The clock will be displayed, but not updated, until you clear the host or it rolls out of view.
Prompts you for confirmation before running the cmdlet.
Shows what would happen if the cmdlet runs. The cmdlet is not run.
Prompts you for confirmation before running the cmdlet.
Shows what would happen if the cmdlet runs. The cmdlet is not run.
Stop a running PSClock.
Use this command to stop a running PSClock from the PowerShell prompt. You can also right-click the clock to dismiss it, or close and remove the runspace it is using.

If you close the PowerShell session that launched the clock, the clock will also be closed. Note that this forced closing will not delete the flag file which indicates that a clock is running. The next time you try to start a clock you may see a warning. Delete the specified file and try starting a clock again.
Prompts you for confirmation before running the cmdlet.
Shows what would happen if the cmdlet runs. The cmdlet is not run.
Prompts you for confirmation before running the cmdlet.
Shows what would happen if the cmdlet runs. The cmdlet is not run.
Stop a running title clock.
Stop a clock running the session title or window tab. This will restore the original title and remove the global timeClockSettings variable.
Prompts you for confirmation before running the cmdlet.
Shows what would happen if the cmdlet runs. The cmdlet is not run.
Prompts you for confirmation before running the cmdlet.
Shows what would happen if the cmdlet runs. The cmdlet is not run.

dev:remarks.maml:para: This will restore the original title and remove the global $timeClockSettings variable.
